key_insight: Prototype-guided latent alignment lets a pretrained molecular foundation model reuse chemically similar source-domain interactions during fine-tuning, improving out-of-distribution generalisation in low-data settings.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical route to adapt ML interatomic potentials to new materials chemistries with fewer expensive DFT labels, making model deployment more data-efficient.
method: Group atoms by similar latent chemical environments to define source-domain prototypes.
Align each target atom’s energy contribution to the nearest matching prototype in latent space.
Use this prototype-based inductive bias to regularise fine-tuning without assuming target chemistry similarity a priori.
evidence_signal: The method was evaluated on rMD17 with MACE and SchNet across varying data budgets, and on MACE-OFF foundation models with SPICE, reducing energy MAE by up to 18% over standard fine-tuning in low-data regimes.
watch_item: Prototype alignment may depend on the quality of latent representations and prototype matching, so performance could weaken if the target domain is highly novel or if clustering is unstable.
paper_title: Prototype-Guided Latent Alignment for Data-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Molecular Foundation Models
